narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 72.7% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger projected outcomes to maintain high pass rates. For you, a Black male applicant, the model's decision hinged most heavily on your performance in later undergraduate years, specifically your Year 3 grades placing you in the 2nd decile within your school—far below the typical 5th or 6th decile for most students. This low ranking in Year 3 was the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, suggesting to the model a potential decline or inconsistency in your academic momentum as you approached graduation. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 3rd place, also well below the average 5th or 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about your early foundational performance compared to peers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 exceeded the typical 3.21 average, which helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success and softened the overall risk assessment. Your solid LSAT score of 36, close to the 36.7 average, and upper-middle family income bracket played supporting roles but weren't decisive enough to offset the academic decile concerns. As a full-time student like over 90% of applicants, that aspect aligned with the norm and didn't sway things either way. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows stronger points like GPA and LSAT but weaker decile rankings, which the model weighed as red flags for long-term exam readiness. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many students from similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements—but the model focuses on patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted GPA enhancement, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends. You might also seek bar prep resources early or consult advisors familiar with paths for Black law aspirants who've overcome similar hurdles. With focused effort on those academic consistencies, your other strengths could shine brighter in a future review.
